title: Day 14: Repair Week — What You've Built

steps.title: You Did the Hard Work
steps.body:
Repair Week. You showed up for seven days and did the work most couples never do.

Let's name what you've built this week:

• Day 8 — You committed to moving from diagnosis to action
• Day 9 — You learned the do-over, the power of resetting a bad moment
• Day 10 — You faced the conversations you'd been avoiding
• Day 11 — You practiced listening without getting defensive
• Day 12 — You learned what a real apology looks like
• Day 13 — You set boundaries that protect instead of push away

That's not a list of skills. That's a repair toolkit. And you own it now.

Today, we consolidate. You'll look back at what changed and set yourself up for Week 3 — Connection.

You can't undo the past.
But you can repair the future.

Every do-over, every hard conversation, every real apology
is a brick in a bridge you're building back to each other.

questions.label:
Think back to Day 8. What felt different about your marriage then vs now?

Be specific:
• Did you have a conversation you've been avoiding?
• Did you use a do-over in a moment of conflict?
• Did you receive hard feedback without getting defensive?
• Did you give or receive a real apology?

Even one small win is real progress. Name it.

steps.question: Which repair skill from this week feels most valuable to keep practicing?
options: The do-over — stopping bad moments before they escalate
Soft startups — beginning hard conversations better
Listening without defending — staying curious under fire
Real apologies — owning my part fully
feedback: The do-over is a game-changer. It stops small fires from becoming four-alarm blazes. Keep using it — even when it feels awkward.
Soft startups are the preventive medicine. A better beginning means fewer messes to clean up. Keep practicing those first ten seconds.
This is the hardest skill of all, and the most powerful. Keep the 'tell me more' muscle flexing. It will serve you for a lifetime.
Real apologies are where trust gets rebuilt brick by brick. Keep using that three-part structure until it's automatic.

steps.title: Repair Is a Practice, Not a Destination
steps.body:
Here's the truth about repair work: you'll never be 'done.'

There will always be moments you handle poorly. There will always be words you wish you could take back. There will always be times you get defensive instead of curious.

That's not failure. That's being human.

The goal isn't perfection. The goal is that your defaults keep shifting.

• That defensiveness gets a millisecond slower and curiosity gets a millisecond faster.
• That do-overs become more natural than pushing through.
• That 'I'm sorry' means something real because you've practiced it.

You're not fixing your marriage once. You're building a practice that will serve it for years.

questions.label:
Rate yourself 1–5 on each repair skill before this week vs after this week:

Skill               | Before | After
Calling a do-over   |  __/5  |  __/5
Soft startups       |  __/5  |  __/5
Listening w/o defense|  __/5  |  __/5
Real apologies      |  __/5  |  __/5
Setting boundaries  |  __/5  |  __/5

Which skill improved the most? Which still needs practice?

steps.title: What's Coming: Connection Week
steps.body:
Week 2 was about repair — mending the cracks so the structure is sound.

Week 3 is about Connection — building the intimacy, friendship, and emotional closeness that makes a marriage not just functional but fulfilling.

You'll learn:
• How to rebuild friendship in a busy life
• How emotional intimacy fuels physical intimacy (and vice versa)
• How to stay curious about each other — even after years together
• How to create shared meaning and rituals

Fix what's broken, then build something beautiful on top.

steps.title: The Repaired Foundation
steps.body:
Imagine a house where the foundation had cracks. You spent a week patching them — not perfectly, but meaningfully.

Now those cracks aren't growing wider. The house is stable.

That's what this week was. You can't make the cracks disappear. But you've stopped them from spreading. And now you can start building the rooms you actually want to live in.

Connection Week starts tomorrow. You need a stable foundation first. You built it. Now comes the good part.

steps.title: Your Week 2 Legacy
steps.prompt:
Write a one-sentence summary of what repair means to you now.

Example: 'Repair means I can mess up and still come back.'
'Or: 'Repair is the bridge between rupture and deeper connection.'

Keep this somewhere you'll see it. When you have a bad moment next week, this sentence will remind you that repair is always available.

steps.title: Achievement Unlocked: The Repairer
steps.body:
You completed Repair Week. You picked up the tools and started mending. Not by pretending nothing was wrong — by naming it, facing it, and doing the work.

Badge earned: The Repairer

steps.title: Day Complete
steps.body: Great work today! You're building something real.
